summary: In 1974, I gave a speech to the Southern Regional Council where I described myself as an oral historian, an old man of the tribe telling legends. The Southern Regional Council inspired local interracial groups in South Carolina, including one I was involved with in Conway. It was a significant organization that influenced efforts toward racial understanding and cooperation during that time.

My family lived in a community that included Edgefield County, South Carolina, where my grandparents and ancestors resided. This county was part of a slave-owning settlement that produced cotton, so my family likely owned slaves. It was a rural area with farms and small businesses, shaping much of my early family history.

Soluda County, South Carolina, was another place where my grandparents lived, alongside Edgefield County. Though I know little about my ancestors there, it was part of the same community and environment that influenced my family's history. The area was rural and agricultural, reflective of the South during that era.

My father, Preston Lafayette Wright, was probably named after the general Lafayette. He served in the Confederate States Army and was a merchant and farmer who established a small bank in Johnston, South Carolina. His life and work were deeply rooted in the South's history and economy during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

My father served in the Confederate States Army, linking our family to the Civil War era. This connection to the Confederacy was part of our heritage, with my grandfather likely going back to Lafayette's day. It shaped much of the family's identity and history in South Carolina.

Johnston, South Carolina, was where my father established the first small bank with a capital of about $25,000. It was also the place where my mother helped found the Methodist Church. Johnston was central to my family's business and religious life in the early 20th century.

My mother attended a girls' school in Georgia, which I believe was in Milledgeville. This was many years ago, but it influenced her strong advocacy for education. She later taught black cooks and others, showing her commitment to learning despite the social restrictions of the time.

The Emancipation Proclamation marked a turning point in laws about teaching black people, which my mother was involved in despite earlier restrictions. She taught black cooks and others, possibly risking legal trouble before the proclamation made such education more acceptable. It symbolized a shift in racial and educational opportunities during Reconstruction.

I have a brother named Preston Lambuth Wright, named after Lambuth, a Methodist missionary active in church affairs. Our mother was very pious and involved in founding the Methodist Church in Johnston, South Carolina. This family connection highlights our deep ties to Methodist religious life.

My mother was one of the founders of the Methodist Church in Johnston, South Carolina, reflecting her strong religious convictions. I attended the church's hundredth anniversary recently, which was a significant event for our family. The Methodist Church played a central role in our community and family identity.

My middle name, Allen, comes from a black bishop named Allen who founded the Afro-Methodist Episcopal Church. This church was admired by my mother, who had a liberal attitude for her time in naming me after a Negro minister. The Afro-Methodist Episcopal Church and its founder inspired her and influenced our family's values.

Allen University in Columbia, South Carolina, is named after the same Bishop Allen who founded the Afro-Methodist Episcopal Church. My mother admired this bishop and his work, which influenced her decision to give me the middle name Allen. The university symbolizes the educational aspirations and religious heritage important to my family.

Columbia, South Carolina, was where three of my elder sisters attended Columbia College, a Methodist institution. It was also the location of Allen University, named after the bishop my mother admired. Columbia played a key role in my family's educational and religious life.

Three of my older sisters went to Columbia College in Columbia, South Carolina, which is a Methodist institution. Their education there reflected our family's commitment to learning and faith. Columbia College was an important part of their lives and ours.

I was a law student at the University of South Carolina around 1917 or 1918 and was involved in a student revolt to remove Dr. William S. Currell as president. The university was in decline after the dynamic leadership of Dr. S.C. Mitchell, and we wanted to restore its prestige. This experience was a defining moment in my early activism.

Dr. William S. Currell was the president of the University of South Carolina during my student years, succeeding Dr. S.C. Mitchell. Many students, including myself, felt he lacked the magnetism and leadership to maintain the university's prestige, leading to a petition for his removal. Although he gave a strong speech and stayed on for a while, our opposition marked a significant episode in university history.

Dr. S.C. Mitchell was the dynamic president of the University of South Carolina before Dr. Currell, and under his leadership, the university reached its greatest prestige. His tenure set a high standard that made Dr. Currell's administration seem lacking by comparison. Mitchell was widely admired by students and faculty alike.

Professor Patterson Wardlaw was one of my idols and a respected figure at the University of South Carolina. Years after the student revolt against Dr. Currell, he told me he had warmly approved of our efforts and would have signed the petitions himself. His support was a meaningful affirmation of our cause.

When I moved to Conway, South Carolina, I became involved in a local interracial group inspired by the South Carolina Interracial Commission. Although the group was small, it reflected the growing efforts toward racial cooperation in the state. Conway was an important place for my later civil rights activities.

I became the state President of the South Carolina Interracial Commission shortly after it was formed. I was invited by the previous president, Beverly Herbert, and accepted without hesitation because of my reputation as a radical on racial issues. The commission was a key organization in advancing interracial understanding in South Carolina during that period.

Beverly Herbert was a lawyer and the president of the South Carolina Interracial Commission before me. He called me to ask if I would accept the presidency, assuming I would because of my known stance on racial equality. His invitation marked the start of my leadership in the commission.

Dr. Josiah Morse was my philosophy professor who deeply influenced my beliefs in the equality of mankind. His teachings helped solidify my commitment to racial equality during my college years. I credit him with fostering the fervor that shaped my later activism.

In my youth, I worked as a clerk in a store in Trenton, South Carolina, owned by Walter Wise. There I witnessed a disturbing incident where a well-dressed black man was chased out of the store for using a washbasin, highlighting the racial hostility of the time. This event deeply affected my views on race relations.

Walter Wise was the owner of the store in Trenton where I worked as a clerk. I witnessed him violently confront a black man for using a washbasin, showing his extreme racial prejudice. This incident was a stark example of the racism I observed growing up in the South.

During the incident in Trenton, the black man who was chased out of the store hid behind the Presbyterian Church. The church served as a place of refuge in that tense moment. It was part of the community landscape where these racial tensions played out.

Aiken, South Carolina, was connected by a shuttle train line to Trenton, which brought northern visitors and their black servants for winter hunting. I recall a black man waiting in Trenton to catch this train to Aiken, which was part of the context for the racial incident I witnessed. Aiken represented a point of contact between northern and southern social dynamics.
